title: Pears in Red Wine
subtitle: Whole poached pears in spiced red wine syrup
excerpt: Firm pears simmered whole in a red wine syrup with cinnamon, cloves, and orange zest until deeply coloured and tender. The wine reduces to a glossy, gently spiced sauce. Served cold with the cooking syrup spooned over.
sourceNotes: Pears poached in wine appear in English cookery writing from at least the seventeenth century; the spicing of cinnamon, cloves, and citrus is consistent across Mrs Beeton (1861), §1342, and earlier domestic manuals. The technique, simmering in an acidulated syrup to keep the flesh from browning, is well established across Victorian and Edwardian sources. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) places poached pears in the autumn dessert chapter, a fair marker of how standard the dish had become in Victorian British home cooking. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) places poached pears in the autumn dessert chapter, a fair marker of how standard the dish had become in Victorian British home cooking.

content.text: Firm pears are the requirement here, Conference or Comice pulled from the tree a day before fully ripe. A pear that is already soft when you start will collapse during poaching rather than holding its shape. The wine does two things: it colours the flesh a deep burgundy red and provides the acid that keeps it from browning. Use a wine you would drink, not cooking wine.

The pears need to be fully submerged throughout poaching. If the saucepan is slightly too wide, set a disc of greaseproof paper or a small plate directly on the surface to keep them below the wine. Cook at a very gentle simmer, barely moving, so the flesh softens without breaking.

items.name: Orange
items.prepNote: zest peeled in strips with a peeler, juice squeezed

content.text: Combine {{red-wine}} of red wine, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, the juice and zest strips of {{orange}} orange, and the split vanilla pod if using in a saucepan deep enough to hold the pears upright. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves, then bring to a simmer.

Peel {{pear-conference}} pears, leaving the stalks intact. Lower them into the simmering wine. They should be mostly submerged; if not, add a splash more wine or water. Reduce the heat to its lowest setting and poach very gently for 35, 40 minutes, turning the pears halfway, until a skewer slides into the thickest part of the flesh with no resistance. The colour should be a deep, even burgundy throughout.

Remove the pears and set them in a serving dish or individual bowls. Return the poaching liquid to a medium-high heat and simmer for 10, 12 minutes until it thickens to a light syrup that coats the back of a spoon. Pour the warm syrup over the pears. Cool to room temperature then refrigerate for at least 2 hours before serving cold.

items.fix: Poach longer at a lower simmer. Very large pears may need 50, 55 minutes.
items.cause: The simmer was too vigorous, sealing the outside before the wine could penetrate, or the pears were too large
items.symptom: Pears are still white inside after the full cooking time

items.fix: Use firm, slightly under-ripe pears. Test with a skewer every 5 minutes after the 30-minute mark.
items.cause: The pears were too ripe when poaching began, or cooked too long

items.fix: Simmer the strained liquid more aggressively for 5 more minutes. It should coat a spoon lightly before you pour it over the pears.
items.cause: Insufficient reduction time after removing the pears
items.symptom: Syrup is very thin and watery

content.text: Poached pears in wine are one of the oldest European desserts. The combination shows up in medieval English and French manuscripts. Wine, sugar, and spice turn raw fruit into a table piece. The same idea ran through the Tudor and Stuart years. By the late 1800s, poached pears were a standard household dessert in Britain. Serve them cold with the syrup. They work as a dinner-party starter alongside a sharp cheese. They also work as a pudding with cream.
